title: ‘Palm Springs Is The Year’s Most Fun Movie. Plus: Introducing ‘The Connect’ With Shea Serrano and Jason Concepcion.
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins go full-spoiler on Andy Samberg's time-loop comedy, then Cristin Milioti and Max Barbakow explain how a Palm Springs weekend became a movie, and Shea Serrano and Jason Concepcion preview their new podcast about movies that rhyme with each other.

films.title: Palm Springs
films.note: Andy Samberg wakes up on a wedding day that keeps repeating; when Cristin Milioti's Sarah wanders into the same cave, she gets pulled into the loop too. Sean and Amanda call the multi-person twist the thing that separates it from every other version of this premise, and dig into the guilt, isolation, and long-term-relationship metaphor buried under the hand-job jokes.

films.title: Groundhog Day
films.note: The template everyone measures this against. Amanda points out the difference: Phil Connors is alone in his loop and spends the whole movie trying to get out, where Niles has made peace with his and has to be talked into wanting to leave.

films.title: 50 First Dates
films.note: Cited as the loop movie built around memory loss rather than a repeating day.

films.title: Source Code
films.note: The version that replays the same eight-minute stretch over and over.

films.title: Before I Fall
films.note: The Zoey Deutch version, built around a car accident replaying.

films.title: Happy Death Day
films.note: The slasher take on the same premise.

films.title: Edge of Tomorrow
films.note: The other loop movie the hosts covered on The Rewatchables.

films.title: Hot Rod
films.note: One of two Samberg vehicles Amanda calls genuine cult classics despite flopping on release.

films.title: Popstar: Never Stop Never Stopping
films.note: The other one, a smarter parody than its box office suggested.

films.title: The Wolf of Wall Street
films.note: Where Sean pegs most people know Cristin Milioti, as Leo's first wife.

films.title: While You Were Sleeping
films.note: Peter Gallagher's other credential, per Sean: the go-to TV dad also playing a rom-com dirtbag here.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.guest: Cristin Milioti and Max Barbakow on

films.title: Palm Springs
films.note: Barbakow and Andy Siara wrote it after a Palm Springs bachelor weekend; Milioti describes cutting a two-page black-hole monologue she'd memorized once the cast trusted the emotional logic over the scientific one.

films.title: Jurassic Park
films.note: Barbakow's shorthand for how the small, cheap idea he and Siara dreamed up in film school eventually ballooned into a real production.

films.title: Disclosure
films.note: Milioti's quarantine pick, the Netflix documentary on trans representation on screen.

films.title: Showgirls
films.note: Milioti's 12th-rewatch favorite; she argues Elizabeth Berkley's performance is genuinely avant-garde and that the movie unfairly tanked her career.

films.title: Being There
films.note: Barbakow's Hal Ashby-kick pick, with Peter Sellers.

films.title: Multiplicity
films.note: From Barbakow's Michael Keaton kick. He admits the cloning logic gets about twenty seconds of explanation and nobody minds.

films.title: Batman
films.note: The other Keaton pick from the same kick.

segments.heading: Introducing The Connect
segments.guest: Shea Serrano and Jason Concepcion on
segments.note: a new Ringer podcast where each week they bring a movie apiece on a shared theme

films.title: Office Space
films.note: Jason's pick for the debut theme, workplace friends.

films.title: Scarface
films.note: Shea's pick for the same theme.

films.title: Whiplash
films.note: Jason's mean-mentors pick, Fletcher.

films.title: The Devil Wears Prada
films.note: Shea's mean-mentors pick, Miranda.

films.title: Contact
films.note: Filed under their recurring theme, Matthew McConaughey doesn't understand outer space.

films.title: Interstellar
films.note: The other half of that theme.

films.title: Juice
films.note: Shea's pick for a theme he'd rather not spoil.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Jason's pick for the same one.

films.title: Michael Clayton
films.note: The source of Jason's cafeteria-table pick, Tom Wilkinson's bread-obsessed Arthur, a choice Shea still argues against.

films.title: Rush Hour
films.note: Shea's counter-pick for the same table: Lee and Carter.

films.title: Reservoir Dogs
films.note: Jason's go-to example of a movie he wanted to rewatch immediately: the opening diner scene where nothing happens.

films.title: The Killer
films.note: One of the John Woo titles Jason wants to bring to the show.

films.title: Army of Shadows
films.note: The Jean-Pierre Melville film Jason name-checks in the same breath.

films.title: Mi Vida Loca
films.note: Shea's running campaign to get more Mexican-American cinema on the show.

films.title: Three O'Clock High
films.note: Jason's underrated-80s-teen-comedy pick, brought up as an example of just texting a friend about a movie.

films.title: The Old Guard
films.note: Namechecked in passing as a recent watch.

films.title: Metropolis
films.note: Jason's example of how far back their robot's-my-friend theme goes.

films.title: Salò, or the 120 Days of Sodom
films.note: Jason's example of a movie you watch out of critical obligation, not appetite.

films.title: Armageddon
films.note: One of the Rewatchables episodes Sean cites as evidence of their taste.

films.title: Gladiator
films.note: The other one.

Salò, or the 120 Days of Sodom.title: Salò, or the 120 Days of Sodom
